name: 1. Introduction to Java
description:
# Introduction to Java

Java is a **high-level, class-based, object-oriented** programming language designed by James Gosling at Sun Microsystems in 1995. It follows the principle of **Write Once, Run Anywhere (WORA)**.

---

## How Java Works

Java code goes through a unique compilation process:

1. You write source code in a `.java` file.
2. The Java Compiler (`javac`) converts it into **bytecode** (`.class` file).
3. The **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** interprets or JIT-compiles the bytecode to native machine code.

---

## JDK vs JRE vs JVM

| Component | What it contains | Who needs it? |
|---|---|---|
| **JDK** (Java Development Kit) | JRE + Compiler + Debugger + Tools | Developers |
| **JRE** (Java Runtime Environment) | JVM + Core Libraries | End Users |
| **JVM** (Java Virtual Machine) | Bytecode interpreter + JIT compiler | Runtime Engine |

---

## Your First Java Program

```java
public class HelloWorld {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello, World!");
    }
}
```

**Breaking it down:**
- `public class HelloWorld` — Every Java program must have at least one class. The filename must match the class name.
- `public static void main(String[] args)` — The entry point of any Java application.
- `System.out.println()` — Prints text to the console.

subTopics.name: 1.1 Data Types
subTopics.description:
# Data Types in Java

Java is a **statically-typed** language — every variable must have a declared type before use.

---

## Primitive Data Types (8 types)

| Type | Size | Default | Range | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `byte` | 1 byte | 0 | -128 to 127 | `byte b = 100;` |
| `short` | 2 bytes | 0 | -32,768 to 32,767 | `short s = 30000;` |
| `int` | 4 bytes | 0 | -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647 | `int num = 42;` |
| `long` | 8 bytes | 0L | -2^63 to 2^63-1 | `long big = 9999999999L;` |
| `float` | 4 bytes | 0.0f | ~7 decimal digits | `float pi = 3.14f;` |
| `double` | 8 bytes | 0.0d | ~15 decimal digits | `double e = 2.71828;` |
| `char` | 2 bytes | '\u0000' | 0 to 65,535 (Unicode) | `char ch = 'A';` |
| `boolean` | 1 bit | false | true or false | `boolean flag = true;` |

> **Note:** `long` values must end with `L`, and `float` values must end with `f`.

---

## Reference Data Types
Reference types hold a **reference (memory address)** to the actual object stored on the Heap.

```java
String name = "Java";    // String is a reference type
int[] arr = {1, 2, 3};   // Array is a reference type
```

---

## Type Casting

### Widening (Implicit) — Small to Large
```java
int myInt = 9;
double myDouble = myInt; // Automatic: 9.0
```

### Narrowing (Explicit) — Large to Small
```java
double myDouble = 9.78;
int myInt = (int) myDouble; // Manual: 9 (truncated)
```

subTopics.name: 1.2 Variables & Constants
subTopics.description:
# Variables in Java

A variable is a named container that holds a value in memory.

---

## Types of Variables

### 1. Local Variables
Declared inside a method. Only accessible within that method.
```java
public void greet() {
    String message = "Hello"; // local variable
    System.out.println(message);
}
```

### 2. Instance Variables
Declared inside a class, but outside any method. Each object gets its own copy.
```java
public class Car {
    String color; // instance variable
}
```

### 3. Static Variables
Declared with the `static` keyword. Shared among all instances of a class.
```java
public class Counter {
    static int count = 0; // shared across all objects
}
```

---

## Constants (`final`)
Use the `final` keyword to make a variable unchangeable after assignment.
```java
final double PI = 3.14159;
// PI = 3.0; // ERROR: cannot assign a value to final variable
```

> **Naming Convention:** Constants are written in `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE`.

subTopics.name: 1.3 Operators
subTopics.description:
# Operators in Java

Operators perform operations on variables and values.

---

## 1. Arithmetic Operators
```java
int a = 10, b = 3;
System.out.println(a + b);  // 13 (Addition)
System.out.println(a - b);  // 7  (Subtraction)
System.out.println(a * b);  // 30 (Multiplication)
System.out.println(a / b);  // 3  (Integer Division)
System.out.println(a % b);  // 1  (Modulus/Remainder)
```

## 2. Comparison Operators
```java
System.out.println(10 == 10); // true
System.out.println(10 != 5);  // true
System.out.println(10 > 5);   // true
System.out.println(10 <= 5);  // false
```

## 3. Logical Operators
```java
boolean x = true, y = false;
System.out.println(x && y);  // false (AND)
System.out.println(x || y);  // true  (OR)
System.out.println(!x);      // false (NOT)
```

## 4. Bitwise Operators
```java
int a = 5;  // 0101 in binary
int b = 3;  // 0011 in binary
System.out.println(a & b);  // 1  (AND: 0001)
System.out.println(a | b);  // 7  (OR:  0111)
System.out.println(a ^ b);  // 6  (XOR: 0110)
System.out.println(~a);     // -6 (NOT)
System.out.println(a << 1); // 10 (Left Shift)
System.out.println(a >> 1); // 2  (Right Shift)
```

## 5. Assignment Operators
```java
int x = 10;
x += 5;  // x = 15
x -= 3;  // x = 12
x *= 2;  // x = 24
x /= 4;  // x = 6
x %= 4;  // x = 2
```

## 6. Ternary Operator
```java
int age = 20;
String status = (age >= 18) ? "Adult" : "Minor";
// Result: "Adult"
```

name: 2. Arrays & Strings
description:
# Arrays and Strings

Two of the most commonly used data structures in Java for storing ordered collections of data and text.

subTopics.name: 2.1 Arrays
2.2 Strings
subTopics.description:
# Strings in Java

Strings are **immutable** — once created, their value cannot be changed. Any modification creates a new String object.

---

## String Creation
```java
// String literal (uses String Pool)
String s1 = "Hello";
String s2 = "Hello";
System.out.println(s1 == s2); // true (same reference in pool)

// Using new keyword (creates new object in Heap)
String s3 = new String("Hello");
System.out.println(s1 == s3);      // false (different objects)
System.out.println(s1.equals(s3)); // true (same content)
```

> **Important:** Always use `.equals()` to compare String values, not `==`.

---

## Common String Methods
```java
String str = "Hello, World!";

str.length();          // 13
str.charAt(0);         // 'H'
str.substring(7);      // "World!"
str.substring(0, 5);   // "Hello"
str.toUpperCase();     // "HELLO, WORLD!"
str.toLowerCase();     // "hello, world!"
str.trim();            // Removes leading/trailing whitespace
str.contains("World"); // true
str.indexOf("World");  // 7
str.replace("World", "Java"); // "Hello, Java!"
str.split(", ");       // ["Hello", "World!"]
str.isEmpty();         // false
str.startsWith("He");  // true
str.endsWith("!");     // true
```

---

## String Comparison
```java
String a = "apple";
String b = "APPLE";
a.equals(b);           // false
a.equalsIgnoreCase(b); // true
a.compareTo(b);        // positive (lexicographic comparison)
```

subTopics.name: 2.3 StringBuilder & StringBuffer
subTopics.description:
# Mutable Strings

Since `String` is immutable, repeatedly modifying it creates many temporary objects (bad for performance). Use `StringBuilder` or `StringBuffer` instead.

---

## StringBuilder (Not thread-safe, faster)
```java
StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder("Hello");
sb.append(" World");       // "Hello World"
sb.insert(5, ",");         // "Hello, World"
sb.replace(0, 5, "Hi");    // "Hi, World"
sb.delete(2, 4);           // "Hi World"
sb.reverse();              // "dlroW iH"
System.out.println(sb.toString());
```

## StringBuffer (Thread-safe, slower)
Exact same API as StringBuilder, but all methods are `synchronized`.
```java
StringBuffer sbf = new StringBuffer("Thread");
sbf.append(" Safe");
// Use when multiple threads modify the same string
```

---

## When to Use What?

| Scenario | Use |
|---|---|
| Few or no modifications | `String` |
| Many modifications, single thread | `StringBuilder` |
| Many modifications, multiple threads | `StringBuffer` |

---

## Real-World Example: Building a CSV Row
```java
StringBuilder csv = new StringBuilder();
String[] values = {"Alice", "25", "Engineer"};
for (int i = 0; i < values.length; i++) {
    csv.append(values[i]);
    if (i < values.length - 1) csv.append(",");
}
System.out.println(csv.toString()); // Alice,25,Engineer
```

name: 3. Object-Oriented Programming
description:
# Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)

Java is fundamentally an OOP language. Everything revolves around **objects** (instances of classes).

---

## The 4 Pillars of OOP

1. **Encapsulation** — Wrapping data and methods together, restricting direct access.
2. **Inheritance** — One class inherits fields and methods from another.
3. **Polymorphism** — One interface, many implementations.
4. **Abstraction** — Hiding complex details, showing only what's necessary.

subTopics.name: 3.1 Classes, Objects & Constructors
3.2 Encapsulation
3.3 Inheritance
3.4 Polymorphism
3.5 Interfaces & Abstract Classes
3.6 Keywords: static, final, this, super

name: 4. Exception Handling
description:
# Exception Handling

Exceptions are runtime errors that disrupt the normal flow of your program. Java provides a structured mechanism to handle them gracefully.

subTopics.name: 4.1 Exception Hierarchy & Types
subTopics.description:
# Exception Hierarchy

All exceptions in Java extend from `java.lang.Throwable`.

---

## Hierarchy
- **Throwable**
  - **Error** — Serious problems you shouldn't try to handle (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`).
  - **Exception**
    - **Checked Exceptions** — Must be handled at compile time (e.g., `IOException`, `SQLException`).
    - **RuntimeException (Unchecked)** — Occur at runtime (e.g., `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`).

---

## Common Exceptions

| Exception | When it occurs |
|---|---|
| `NullPointerException` | Accessing a member on a null reference |
| `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Accessing an invalid array index |
| `NumberFormatException` | Parsing an invalid number from a string |
| `ClassCastException` | Invalid type cast |
| `ArithmeticException` | Division by zero |
| `FileNotFoundException` | File doesn't exist |
| `IllegalArgumentException` | Method receives an invalid argument |

subTopics.name: 4.2 Try-Catch-Finally
4.3 Throw & Custom Exceptions
